questions.concept_description: General Guidelines for Initial Haz-Mat Response
questions.question_text: True or False: If you suspect a hazardous-materials release on a roadside, you should quickly approach the spill to “size it up,” then decide whether to call it in.
questions.answer: False – approach cautiously only with authorization; isolate the area first, keep people at a safe distance, avoid contact/inhalation, and maintain communications. Rushing in increases risk and can make you part of the problem. (pp. 507–509).

questions.concept_description: Six Clues for Detecting Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text: Fill in the blank: One prioritized clue is the shape of the \_\_\_\_\_\_ used to store or transport the material, which can hint at what’s inside.
questions.answer: container. Container shapes (e.g., tankers) help identify likely contents; the six clues are prioritized, starting with occupancy/location, then container shapes. (pp. 512–514).

questions.question_text:
While scouting a wildland–urban interface, you notice a small LPG cylinder with a turquoise-colored valve stem near a shed. What does this most strongly indicate?  
 **A.** A standard propane cylinder that has weathered normally  
 **B.** A cylinder previously used for chlorine storage  
 **C.** A cylinder containing or having contained anhydrous ammonia used in drug production  
 **D.** A harmless camping fuel canister you can move aside
questions.answer: C – a turquoise/oxidized brass valve on a small “LPG” tank indicates anhydrous ammonia commonly tied to clandestine meth labs; do not touch and report immediately. A: normal weathering does not mandate turquoise oxidation; B: chlorine is stored differently; D: never move suspect cylinders. (pp. 510–512).

questions.question_text:
Brief scenario MCQ: You detect a strong chemical odor near a shed and see a large LPG tank within \~100 ft of a structure. After Detect and Estimate, what should be your immediate response objective?  
 **A.** Enter upwind with a partner to close valves  
 **B.** Stay away, keep a safe distance, notify authorities, prevent others from entering  
 **C.** Don structural PPE and attempt product control  
 **D.** Wait for odor to dissipate, then re-check
questions.answer: B – “Choose the response objective”: stay away, maintain distance, notify, and keep others out. A/C: operational control isn’t an awareness-level action; D delays protective measures. (p. 510).

questions.concept_description: Six Clues for Detecting Hazardous Materials
questions.question_text:
Multiple-choice: You pull up to a collision involving a road tanker near a school. To size up the hazard, which clue should you consider first according to the prioritized list?  
 **A.** Markings and colors on containers  
 **B.** Type of occupancy and location  
 **C.** Your sense of smell  
 **D.** Shipping papers
questions.answer: B – occupancy/location is the first-priority clue; here, a school drives immediate protective decisions. A/D come later; C: senses can mislead and may be dangerous with toxic/odorless gases. (pp. 512–514).

questions.question_text: True or False: If you discover suspected UXO while line scouting, it’s acceptable to key your handheld radio to report it as long as you remain 500 feet away.
questions.answer: False – never transmit on a radio around UXO; stop, do not move closer, mark the area clearly, keep at least 500 ft away, and report the discovery to your supervisor by safe means. (pp. 514–515).

questions.question_text:
Decision-making MCQ: In an abandoned motel unit near the fire edge you observe solvent cans, large quantities of cold-med blister packs, and a small cylinder with a turquoise valve. Using D.E.C.I.D.E., which initial sequence best fits your role?  
 **A.** Detect → Enter to shut off sources → Choose “rescue/neutralize”  
 **B.** Detect → Estimate likely harm → Choose “stay away/keep others out” → Notify authorities  
 **C.** Detect → Identify action options → Do “ventilate room” → Evaluate  
 **D.** Detect → Do “remove cylinder to safe area” → Evaluate
questions.answer: B – After detection, estimate harm, then choose a defensive objective (stay away, isolate, notify) and keep others out; drug labs pose explosion/toxic risks. A/C/D involve entry or manipulation, which are unsafe at awareness level. (pp. 510–513).

questions.question_text:
A dozer boss flags “metal objects” on a former range. Crews suggest moving debris so the dozer can pass. What is the safest integrated action set for you to direct?  
 **A.** Move fragments gently with hand tools; call EOD if fuses are visible  
 **B.** Back the dozer out; mark the area; no radio transmission near the site; maintain ≥500-ft standoff; report to supervisor  
 **C.** Photograph items up close, then post a safety alert  
 **D.** Use the dozer to push items into a pile for later pickup
questions.answer: B – Stop and do not move closer, clearly mark, keep ≥500 ft away, avoid radio transmission near UXO, and report to supervision for proper disposal. A/C/D increase detonation risk. (pp. 514–515).

questions.concept_description: General Guidelines for Initial Haz-Mat Response
questions.question_text:
Multiple-choice scenario: You arrive first to a roadside puddle of unknown chemical with bystanders walking toward it and a breeze pushing vapors across the lane. Which action package best matches recommended guidelines?  
 **A.** Enter quickly to identify the product; reassure bystanders; move fire apparatus closer for lighting  
 **B.** Isolate the area and direct people upwind/upgrade to a safe distance; avoid walking into/through the spill or vapors; maintain comms with supervisor/dispatch  
 **C.** Attempt to neutralize the liquid with dirt while you wait for haz-mat specialists  
 **D.** Ignore until a confirmed placard or shipping paper is found
questions.answer:
B – First isolate and keep the public away, stay upwind/upgrade, avoid contact/inhalation, and maintain communications; do not rush in or manipulate the product. A/C/D violate “do not touch,” “avoid vapors,” and the need to act defensively early. (pp. 507–509). 

**\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

questions.question_text: Fill in the blank: When you need to move past coworkers who are actively swinging tools on the fireline, you should call out “\_\_\_\_\_\_” and wait until they pause before passing.
questions.answer: Coming through\! – This alerts others to stop swinging so you can pass without entering their tool arc; it’s a specific, taught phrase, not casual chatter. p. 15-7;

questions.question_text:
You’re traversing uneven ground with an axe. What is the correct way to carry it to minimize injury risk and maintain control?  
 **A.** Grip the handle end; let the head trail behind with the handle angled up  
 **B.** Hold it at the balance point just behind the head, head facing forward, handle parallel to the ground  
 **C.** Sling it over a shoulder with the blade behind you  
 **D.** Hold the bit in your gloved hand with the edge pointing down
questions.answer: B – Holding at the balance point with the head forward keeps control and reduces fatigue; the other methods are unstable or unsafe. Rebuttals: A—poor control; C—exposes others; D—never hold by the bit. p. 15-11;

questions.question_text:
When carrying a McLeod along the line, which orientation best reduces the chance of puncture injuries if you fall?  
 **A.** Hoe edge down to cut while walking  
 **B.** Hoe edge up so you won’t fall on the rake tines  
 **C.** Rake tines forward for quick deployment  
 **D.** Blade vertical to fit narrow trails
questions.answer: B – Carrying hoe edge up avoids falling onto the tines; punctures are more dangerous than lacerations. Rebuttals: A—creates trip hazard; C—exposes tines; D—doesn’t address injury risk. p. 15-17;

questions.question_text:
You’re showing a rookie how to file a Pulaski. Which guidance is correct for both edge quality and safety?  
 **A.** Use a power grinder; press hard on both strokes  
 **B.** Use a flat mill-bastard file with guard; wear gloves; make long forward strokes, keep a constant angle, release pressure on the backstroke  
 **C.** Short, fast strokes changing angles to “chase” nicks  
 **D.** File toward your body so you can see the bevel
questions.answer: B – Forward-only pressure with a guarded mill-bastard file, constant angle, and gloves yields a safe, consistent edge. Rebuttals: A—risks ruining tool; C—inconsistent bevel; D—increases injury risk. p. 15-5;

questions.question_text:
Light grass is racing upslope. Two firefighters must cut a quick scratch line with McLeods. Which setup is correct?  
 **A.** Both chop vertically like garden hoes  
 **B.** Lead faces travel with tool behind, scraping edge down; second trails with tool in front, rake tines intertwined atop the first, adding downward pressure  
 **C.** Lead rakes; second scrapes randomly for speed  
 **D.** Both pull backward to keep faces away from heat
questions.answer: B – Intertwined tools let the lead provide motion while the rear adds cutting pressure—fast for emergency scratch line. Rebuttals: A—wastes energy; C—uncoordinated; D—awkward/unsafe. p. 15-18;

questions.question_text:
A rigid backpack tank starts denting inward and the stream weakens even though it’s full. What single check fixes the problem first?  
 **A.** Tighten all hose clamps  
 **B.** Open/clear the air vent in the tank lid  
 **C.** Switch to spray tip for less backpressure  
 **D.** Add fuel to the engine
questions.answer: B – A plugged lid vent causes vacuum and tank collapse; clearing it restores flow. (Manual backpumps have no engine.) Rebuttals: A/C—symptoms are vacuum, not leaks; D—N/A. p. 15-28;

questions.question_text:
While chainsawing a leaning tree, the kerf starts to close. Which choice manages the cut and team safety?  
 **A.** Drive a metal wedge; keep cutting solo to finish fast  
 **B.** Insert plastic wedges; designate a lookout and use a preplanned escape route  
 **C.** Use a wooden wedge; continue without a lookout to reduce noise  
 **D.** Pry the kerf with the bar to avoid carrying wedges
questions.answer: B – Plastic wedges prevent chain damage/kickback with saws, and a lookout plus escape route are required risk controls. Rebuttals: A—metal damages chain; C—no lookout is unsafe; D—prying risks bar/kerf failure. p. 15-24 (wedges), p. 15-23 (lookout/escape);

questions.question_text:
During a burn-out in light grass with frequent spots, which combination best controls spread while conserving water?  
 **A.** Random blasts ahead of the flame; keep walking  
 **B.** Deploy pumps sparingly; use wide fog only  
 **C.** Deploy pumps liberally; sweep parallel to the edge in a coordinated motion; keep nozzle hand steady and pump with the other; add wetting agent to each tank  
 **D.** Aim high arcs over the line to “rain” water down
questions.answer: C – Parallel sweeping coordinated with pumping controls the edge; wetting agent stretches water; steady/nozzle hand improves aim. Rebuttals: A—wastes water; B—too little coverage; D—poor placement/splash. p. 15-29–15-30;

questions.question_text:
Your crew is cutting handline on a steep side-hill. Which set of practices is correct?  
 **A.** Carry tools on uphill side; 6-ft spacing; pass silently to avoid distractions  
 **B.** Carry tools on downhill side; ≥10-ft spacing; announce “Coming through\!” and wait for swings to stop before passing  
 **C.** Carry tools vertically; any spacing works if PPE is worn; pass whenever there’s a gap  
 **D.** Carry tools downhill; 3-ft spacing to stay tight; pass quickly without stopping
questions.answer:
B – Downhill carry prevents landing on a sharp edge if you fall; maintain at least 10 feet between workers; use the taught pass-through call and wait. Rebuttals: A/C/D—each violates one or more safety rules. p. 15-7–15-8;
